labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A1.1 Water > A1.1.6 Physical properties of water and the consequences for animals in aquatic habitats
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A1.1.6 Physical properties of water and the consequences for animals in aquatic habitats
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question specifically compares physical properties like buoyancy, viscosity, thermal conductivity, and specific heat capacity between water and air, which is the exact focus of this topic, often illustrated using the Ringed Seal and Black-throated Loon as standard examples.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B4.1 Adaptation to environment > B4.1.2 Adaptations of organisms to the abiotic environment of their habitat

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explores how the physical properties of the abiotic environment (water vs. air) determine the specific challenges and consequences for the organisms living there, which is a fundamental aspect of adaptation to an environment.

options.content: Water’s buoyancy supports the seal, but higher viscosity increases drag during swimming.
options.markscheme: This is correct. Because water is denser than air, it provides higher buoyancy, allowing large aquatic animals to stay afloat easily. However, its higher viscosity creates more resistance (drag), requiring streamlined bodies for efficient movement.

options.content: Water conducts heat less than air, so the seal loses heat more slowly than the loon.
options.markscheme: This is incorrect. Water has a much higher thermal conductivity than air (approximately 28 times higher), meaning it conducts heat away from the body far more rapidly. Seals require thick blubber to insulate against this rapid heat loss.

options.content: Water has lower specific heat capacity than air, so oceans change temperature more rapidly.
options.markscheme: This is incorrect. Water has a much higher specific heat capacity than air ($4.18\text{ J}\ \text{g}^{-1}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ vs $\approx 1.0\text{ J}\ \text{g}^{-1}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$), which means it absorbs more energy before changing temperature, making aquatic environments thermally stable.

options.content: Air has higher buoyancy and viscosity than water, so the loon generates lift more easily.
options.markscheme: This is incorrect. Air has much lower viscosity and lower buoyancy than water. The loon must use significant energy to generate lift because air provides very little buoyant support compared to the seal's marine environment.

parts.content: Outline the role of xylem tissue in the transport of water in plants.
parts.markscheme:
- Xylem transports water and mineral ions from roots to leaves/shoots <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transport is in an upward direction (root to shoot) in the transpiration stream <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Xylem vessels are dead/hollow and form continuous tubes for mass flow <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Lignified/thickened walls help prevent collapse under tension/negative pressure <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how transpiration generates a pull that moves water up the xylem.
parts.markscheme:
- Water evaporates from moist cell walls of mesophyll into air spaces (in the leaf) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Water vapour diffuses out through stomata down a concentration gradient <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Evaporation reduces water potential in leaf air spaces/mesophyll so water moves from xylem to replace it <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This creates tension/negative pressure (transpiration pull) in the xylem <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Tension is transmitted down the continuous water column to the roots, drawing water up <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how cohesion and adhesion enable the transport of water from roots to leaves.
parts.markscheme:
- Cohesion is the attraction between water molecules <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cohesion enables formation of a continuous water column in xylem vessels <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Water molecules form hydrogen bonds with each other <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Adhesion is the attraction between water molecules and xylem vessel walls <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Adhesion helps counteract gravitational force <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Both forces enable capillary action in xylem vessels <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Capillary action assists upward movement of water <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This creates continuous transport of water from roots to leaves <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award marks for clear explanation of how these forces work together in the transport process</note>
